summary:
The White Temple at [[event:1618|Uruk]], constructed around 3200-3100 BCE atop the Anu Ziggurat (a massive mudbrick platform approximately 13 meters high), was one of the earliest and most impressive examples of elevated temple architecture in Mesopotamia. Named by modern archaeologists for the whitewashed plaster finish of its exterior walls, the temple was dedicated to the sky god Anu, one of the principal deities of the Sumerian pantheon. Its position on a raised platform — a forerunner of the great ziggurats of later Mesopotamian civilization — may have symbolized the meeting point between earth and the divine heavens.

The White Temple itself was a rectangular mudbrick structure approximately 22 by 17 meters, built in the tripartite plan typical of early Mesopotamian temples: a central hall flanked by side rooms. An altar and offering table occupied the central space where rituals were performed. The temple was reached by a stairway or ramp ascending the Anu Ziggurat platform, separating the sacred precinct from the surrounding urban space and reinforcing the visual and symbolic elevation of divine worship above the city.

The White Temple represents an early articulation of architectural principles that would define Mesopotamian sacred architecture for millennia: the elevation of the temple on a platform, the use of whitewash to create a gleaming visual landmark visible from a distance, and the tripartite interior plan. The ziggurat tradition, of which the Anu platform was a forerunner, reached its fullest expression in structures like the great ziggurat of Ur built a thousand years later, which still survives to a substantial height. The White Temple was eventually buried under successive rebuildings, as was customary, preserving it for later excavation.
